content.full_text:
Re:
Farn PolrrrcAI- PRACTTcES CouvrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5ffi . Fax (916) 322-0886
June 17,2A04
Richard A. Rogan
Jeffer Mangels Butler & Marmaro, LLP
Two Embarcadero Center, 5ft Floor
San Francisco, CA 94lll-3824
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-109
Dear Mr. Rogan:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf Dean J. Evans for advice
regarding the post-governmental employment provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act').I Our advice is based on the facts presented in your request; the Commission does
not act as a finder of fact when it provides advice. (In re Oslesby (1975) I FPPC Ops.
7r.)
QUESTION
Is Mr. Evans prohibited by the post-employment provisions of the Act from
appearing before his former state administrative agency employer, the California Public
Utilities Commission ("CPUC'), in his capacity as a court-appointod receiver, in order to
obtain CPUC approval of the court-ordered sale of the assets of a regulated water utility?
CONCLUSION
The "one-year ban" prohibits Mr. Evans from appearing before or cornmunicating
with the CPUC in his capacity as a court-appointed receiver in order to obtain approval o1
the court-ordered sale and transfer of a water utility's assets. The courts are neither a
state agency nor a local government agency, as defined by the Act. For this reason, an
individual acting in apaid capacityto represent a court before the CPUC does not qualifu
under the exceptions to the one-year ban found in section 87406(e).
With respect to the permanent ban, it does not appear from the facts provided that
the instant proceeding to obtain CPUC approval for the sale and transfer of water utility
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl}l4. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. 4-04-109
Page No. 2
assets is a proceeding in which Mr. Evans personally and substantially participated while
in the employ of the CPUC. If such is the case, the permanurt ban would not apply. In
any event, since the courts are a constitutionally-recognized branch of California state
goveilrment, Mr. Evans's appearances or cornmrmications in this regard will be on behalf
of the State of California and for that reason, will not be zubject to the "permanent ban.,,
FAC"TS
Dean Evans retired from the CPUC on August l,2OA3. Duringhis tenure at the
CPUC, he served as director of the water division and thereafter as an administrative law
judge.
Following retirernent from the CPUC, Mr. Evans was appointed by the Superior
Court of California for the County of Monterey ("Corrt') as a receiver in the matter of
Alisal Water Corporation, doing business as Alco WaterSerrrice (*Alco'). Among other
thirgt, the Court is requiring Mr- Evans to transfer and sell certain assets belonging to
Alco. Pursuant to section 851 of the Public Utilities Code the transfer and sale of these
assets is subject to the prior approval of the CPUC. (Henpy v. public Utilities
C ommiss ion (1961) 56 Cal.zd 21 4.)
ANALYSIS
State administrative officials2 who leave state service are subject to two types of
post-govemmental employment restrictions under the Act:
o A "one-yearban" prohibiting a state employee from
communicating with his or her former ag€xlcy to influence the agency,s
administrative or legislative action (section 37406); and
o A '?ermanent ban" barring a state employee from *switching sides?,
in any specific proceding on which the employee worked while in state
senrice (sections 87 4OO-87 405).
A. The OneYear Ban
Section 87406(dxl) states in pertinent part:
'T.lo designated employee of a state administrative agcncy, anyofficer,
ernployee, or consultant of a state adminiskative agency who holds a
position which entails the making or participation in the making of
decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any financial
interest, and no member of a state administative agency, for a period of
2 A "state administrative official" is defined in section S7400(b) as "every member, officer,
employee or consultant of a state administrative agency who as part ofhis or her official responsibilities
engages in anyjudicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding in other than a purely clerical, secretarial or
ministeri al cap acily."


FileNo. A-04-109
Page No. 3
one year after leaving office or employment, shall, for compensation, act as
agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person, by making
any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written
communication, before any state administrative agency, or officer or
employee thereof, for which he or she worked or represented dwing the 12
months before leaving office or employment, if the appearumce or
communication is made for the pu{pose of influencing a&ninistrative or
legislative action, or influe,ncing any action orproceeding involving the
issuancg amendment, awarding or revocation of a permit, license, gfant"
or contract, or the sale orpurchase of goods orpropert5r."
In his capacity as an administrative lawjudge and as directorofthe CPUC's water
division, Mr. Evans served in positions designated in the CPUC's conflict of interest
code.3 In addition, he is being compensated forhis duties as a corrt-appointod receiver,
including any appearances or communications which he might make before the CPUC.
These appearances and commrrrications, ifmadg would be forthepnrpose of obtaining
the CPUC's approval for the sale and transfer of certain Aloo assets. As such, these
appearances and communications will be forthepurpose ofinfluencing an action or
proceeding involving ttte "issuancg ame,ndment, awarding orrevocation of apermit,
licanse, grffit, or contract, orthe sale orpurchase of goods orproffiy." (Section
87406(d)(1).) Thus, unless an exception applies, Mr. Evans is prohribited by the one-year
ban from appearing before or communicating with the CPUC, in his capacity as a court-
appointed receiver, for the purpose of obtaining the CPUC's alrproval for the sale and
transfer of assets belonging to Alco.
The exceptions to the one-year ban are found at section 874A6@):
"(e) The prohibitions contained in subdivisions O), (c), and (d) shall
not apply to any individual subject to this section who is orbecomes any
of the following:
(1) An officer or employee of another state agency boar4 or
commission ifthe appearance or communication is forthepurlpose of
influencing legislative or administrative action on behalfof the state
agency, board, or commission.
(2) An official holding an elective office of a local govermnent
agency if the appearance or communication is for the purpose of
influencing legislative or adminisfiative action on behalf ofthe local
government agency."
The Commission has long held that the courts are not considered under the Act to
be either local government agencies, as definod in section &20/,l,or state agencies, as
defined in section 82049. (In re Baty (1979) 5 FPPC Ops. l0; Yicbey Advice Letter, No.
l-99-021; Gomez Advice Letter, No. 4-99-068.) Thus, the exceptions at section 87aA6@)
3 This information was provided in a telephone conversation with Commission staff.


File No. A-04-109
Page No. 4
do not apply to Mr. Evans when acting in his capactty as a court-appointed receiver. He
is prohibited by the one-year ban from appearing before his former state administrative
agency employer, the CPUC, in connection with a request for the CPUC's approval to
sell and transfer assets currently owned by Alco.
B. The Permanent Ban
Sections 87401 and 87402 (collectively, the "permane,lrt ban') prohibit a former
state administrative official from advising orrepresenting anyperson, other than the State
of California, for compensation in anyjudicial, quasi-judicial orotherproceeding in
which the official participated while in state service, or from assisting another who will
engage in this activity. An official is considered to have "participated" in aproceeding if
he or she took part in the proceeding 'lersonally and substantially through decision,
approval, disapproval, formal written recornmendation, rendering advice on a substantial
basis, investigation or use of confidential information.-.." ($ S7400(d).)
Thepermane,ntban in sections 874f/l andl74o2applies onlyto judicial,
quasi-judicial, or other proceedings before any court or state a&ninistrative agency in
which a former employee participated while at his or her former agency. Section
87a00(c) defines 'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceodingl' to include:
"...any proceoding application, rquest for a ruling or other determination,
contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other
particulm matter involving a specific party orparties in any corrt or state
administrative agency, including but not limited to any proceoding govemed by
chapter 5 (commencing with Section I1500) of Division 3 of Title 2 of the
Government Code."
An application for approval to sell and hansfer assets of apublic utility is a
particular matter involving a specific party orparties before a state administrative agency
(i.e., the applicant and the public utility crrrently owning the assets) and involves a
request for a ruling or other determination with respect to a proposed disposition of
assets. Thus, a request for CPUC approval to sell and transfer assets currently owned by
Alco will comprise a 'Judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceding3 to which the
permanent ban may potentially apply. However, if a request for such approval is a new
proceeding and will not be considered in connection with any otherproceeding in which
Mr. Evans previously participated personally and substantially, either directly or in
connection with his supenrisory responsibilities as directorofthe CPUC's Water
Division, the permanent ban would not apply.
Significantly, the permanent ban is not higgered when a former state
administrative official's appearance in a proceeding is on behalf of the State of
California. Although the Act does not define, for purposes of the permanent ban, what
organrzational units of govemment may be identified as "the State of California," the
California State Constitution recognizes that the state's govemment is comprised of three
separate components: the executive branch, the Legislaturg and the judiciary. Thus,


FileNo. A-04-109
Page No. 5
broadly invoking the State of California as a client would necessarily include the courts,
as organs of the judiciary. In other words, a "state agenct''as defined rmder section
82049 is a narrower definition - a classification consisting of a subunit of state
Sovemment - as opposed to the permanent ban's stafutory reference to the State of
California, which refers to the state government as a whole.
In light of the abovg Mr Evans is acting as a representative on behalf of the State
of California when performing his official duties as a court-appointed receiver. Thus,
when acting in this apacity he is not subject to the permanent ban.
If you have any other questions regmding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-s66A.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel
oftrtdF\ L,
\
t
By
L. Glick
Counsel, Legal Division
KLGje
